prompt: Which plant snaps shut to catch its prey using tiny trigger hairs — but only after the hairs are touched twice?
The massive African baobab tree is famous for storing what inside its trunk?
What do we call the underground fungal threads that connect tree roots in a forest, sharing nutrients between them?
Rafflesia arnoldii holds the record for the world's largest individual flower. What does it smell like to attract pollinators?
Pando, a single organism in Utah that looks like a whole forest of 47,000 trees, is actually one clonal colony of which species?
The world's oldest known non-clonal living tree, Methuselah, is over 4,800 years old. What kind of tree is it?
Agave plants bloom exactly once in their lifetime, then die. How long do most species wait before flowering?
Which ecosystem stores the most carbon per hectare on Earth, locking away far more than an equivalent area of tropical rainforest?
A single hectare of ancient peatland can lock away how many times more carbon than the same area of tropical rainforest?
The Pando aspen clone in Utah is estimated to be up to 80,000 years old. What is currently its biggest threat?
options: Rising temperatures killing the root system
Deer and elk eating all the new saplings
Invasive bark beetles destroying the stems
Forest fires burning the underground rhizomes

prompt: Welwitschia mirabilis, a plant found in the Namib Desert, typically has only two leaves for its entire life. How long can those leaves live?

The corpse flower (Amorphophallus titanum) is technically the world's largest unbranched inflorescence, not a single flower. What triggers it to heat up — sometimes to near human body temperature — when it blooms?
options: A burst of photosynthesis in the spathe
Oxidation of stored oils inside the spadix
Fermentation by bacteria living on the pollen

prompt: What gas do plants release into the air as a by-product of photosynthesis?
Peanuts grow in an unusual spot compared with most nuts. Where do they actually ripen?
Sunflowers are known for slowly turning to follow what across the sky during the day?
Which of these is NOT actually a tree, despite often being mistaken for one?
A cactus has spines instead of normal leaves mainly to help it do what?
What green pigment makes most plant leaves their colour and captures light for photosynthesis?
Which fruit's seeds are the tiny dots you can see all over its outer surface?
The fluffy white 'parachutes' that let dandelion seeds drift on the breeze are an example of which kind of seed dispersal?
Bananas grown commercially today are almost all clones of a single variety, making them dangerously vulnerable to disease. What is that variety called?
Vanilla, one of the world's most popular flavours, comes from the seed pod of which kind of plant?
When acacia trees in Africa are nibbled by browsing animals, they can warn nearby trees to ramp up their defences by releasing what?
options: An electric pulse through the soil
A chemical gas into the air

prompt: Which everyday spice is the dried, ground-up bark of a tropical tree?
Mosses and ferns reproduce without flowers or seeds by releasing what instead?
Why does freshly cut grass give off that distinctive 'green' smell?
options: It's a chemical distress signal the grass releases when wounded
Soil bacteria are disturbed by the cut
The blades are releasing extra oxygen

prompt: Most plants use the common 'C3' route to capture carbon, but plants like corn and sugarcane use a more efficient method suited to hot, sunny climates. What is it called?
Many desert succulents open their leaf pores only at night to save water, storing carbon as acid until daytime. What is this water-saving form of photosynthesis called?
Some plants in the pea and bean family enrich the soil with nitrogen thanks to bacteria living in nodules on their roots. What are these bacteria called?
The 'sensitive plant' Mimosa pudica famously folds its leaves the instant you touch it. What mechanism makes the movement happen so fast?
options: An electric spark snaps the leaf shut
Tiny muscles contract in the stem
The leaf releases gas that pushes it closed
Cells at the leaf base rapidly lose water and go limp

prompt: Bamboo of a given species can suddenly flower all at once across an entire region — even plants growing on different continents — then die. Roughly how long is the cycle between these mass flowerings for many species?
Lichens, which grow on rocks and tree bark almost everywhere, are not single organisms but a partnership between a fungus and what?
Each ring you count in the cross-section of a tree trunk usually represents one what?
What is the tallest type of tree alive on Earth today, with the record holders towering over 110 metres?
Bees and other insects are attracted to flowers mainly to collect nectar and what else?
A tomato is, botanically speaking, actually classified as which of these?
The spice cloves are actually the dried, unopened flower buds of a tropical tree. Most people assume they are which part of the plant?
The small holes on the underside of a leaf that open and close to let gases in and out are called what?
Dried and ground from the berries of a climbing vine, which spice was once so prized that it was used as money to pay rent and ransoms?
The coco de mer palm of the Seychelles produces the heaviest seed of any plant, weighing up to around 25 kilograms. What is it famous for resembling?
Some orchids fool male insects into mating with their flowers by mimicking the look and scent of what?
The fierce burning heat of chilli peppers comes from a chemical called capsaicin. Which animals can happily eat the hottest chillies without feeling any burn?
Saffron is the world's most expensive spice by weight largely because it is harvested by hand from which part of a crocus flower?
Pineapples take a surprisingly long time to grow and do not ripen further once picked. Roughly how long does a single pineapple take to mature on the plant?
The strangler fig has an unusual and rather sinister way of growing. How does it typically start life?
options: Floating as a seed on a river
As a seed sprouting high up on another tree

prompt: The seeds of which common roadside plant cling to animal fur with tiny hooks — an idea that directly inspired the invention of Velcro?
The 'humongous fungus' in Oregon's Malheur National Forest is one of the largest living organisms on Earth, sprawling across several square kilometres of forest floor. What type of organism is it?

Many plants in the cabbage and mustard family release a sharp, peppery bite only after their cells are crushed. This happens because crushing mixes a stored compound with an enzyme to make which pungent chemical?
Some species of bamboo are among the fastest-growing plants in the world. Under ideal conditions, how much can certain bamboo grow in a single day?
The walnut and several other trees defend their turf by releasing a chemical that poisons the soil so rival plants can't grow nearby. What is this chemical warfare between plants called?

Resurrection plants like Selaginella can dry out almost completely, looking dead for years, then spring back to life within hours of getting water. Which sugar do many of them use to protect their cells while dehydrated?
Aloe vera is a popular houseplant prized for the clear gel inside its thick leaves. What is that gel most famously used to soothe?
The vast fields of bright yellow flowers that became a national symbol of Ukraine are grown mainly for their oil and edible seeds. Which flower is it?
Which of these popular 'nuts' is not really a nut at all, but the seed of a stone fruit related to the peach?
Poison ivy causes an itchy rash thanks to an oily chemical on its leaves. What is the classic rhyme people use to identify and avoid it?
options: Leaves of three, let it be
Roots that creep, put you to sleep

prompt: The giant Amazon water lily has leaf pads so large and strong they can support the weight of a small child. Its scientific name honours which 19th-century monarch?
Coffee plants make caffeine not to keep us awake but as a natural weapon. What does the caffeine do for the plant?
options: It poisons insects and stunts nearby rival seedlings
It helps the leaves photosynthesise faster
It makes the beans smell sweeter to birds
It draws extra rain toward the roots

prompt: Tulip mania in the 1600s Netherlands saw single rare bulbs sell for the price of a house. The most prized flowers had dramatic flame-like streaks — later found to be caused by what?
options: A rare mineral in the soil

prompt: The ghostly white 'ghost plant' (Monotropa uniflora) has no green colour and does no photosynthesis of its own. How does it get its food?
options: It absorbs sunlight through its white stem
It taps into fungi that are feeding off nearby trees
It lives purely on rainwater and dissolved minerals
It digests insects like a Venus flytrap

prompt: Plants like the dumb cane (dieffenbachia) and rhubarb leaves defend themselves with raphides — microscopic needle-sharp crystals that sting the mouth of anything that bites them. What are these crystals made of?
Cashews are never sold in their shell, and even 'raw' ones have been heat-treated first. Why can't you just crack one open and eat it straight from the shell?
options: The shell holds a caustic oil related to the toxin in poison ivy
The shell is too hard for any tool to crack
The shell traps a gas that spoils the nut instantly
The nut has no flavour at all until it is roasted
